debates.title: Zack Polanski and Sir John Redwood Debate: Should We Tax the Rich More?
common_ground: The UK faces serious fiscal pressures requiring policy response.
Child poverty in the UK is a real and significant problem.
Some form of wealth-related taxation (e.g. a mansion tax) is at least conceivable as a policy option.
Scrapping the two-child benefit cap is a desirable policy outcome.
Behavioural responses by wealthy individuals to taxation are an empirical question that matters for policy design.
UK economic performance, including employment, is a legitimate measure of policy success.

participants.label: SPEAKER_00 (Zack Polanski, Green Party)
participants.position_summary: Advocates a 1% annual wealth tax on assets over £10m and 2% over £1bn, claiming it would raise £14.9bn/year, dismisses capital flight concerns, attacks fossil fuel subsidies in favour of a renewables jobs guarantee, and frames the issue morally around inequality and child poverty.

participants.label: SPEAKER_02 (Sir John Redwood, Conservative)
participants.position_summary: Opposes the wealth tax as unworkable 'magic money tree' thinking that would accelerate capital flight; argues British industry is already leaving due to high taxes, high energy prices, and net-zero policy; advocates £47bn spending cuts to enable lower taxes and higher employment.

participants.label: SPEAKER_03 (Moderator)
participants.position_summary: Presses both sides with empirical challenges, particularly questioning whether annual wealth taxes raise meaningful revenue in comparator countries, citing economists' view that recurring wealth taxes fail due to behavioural change.

participants.label: SPEAKER_01
participants.position_summary: Intervenes to challenge SPEAKER_02 on Conservative tax record and possible drift toward Reform.

debates.title: Wealth tax: James O’Brien vs LBC caller ‘who comes from nothing’
common_ground: The UK government faces serious fiscal and public-service pressures that require some response.
The Duke of Westminster, whose fortune is inherited, could legitimately be subject to a wealth tax.
The UK currently has a top marginal income tax rate of 45%.
Wealthy individuals can and sometimes do relocate to lower-tax jurisdictions.
Reducing waste and improving management of public spending is, in principle, desirable.

participants.label: SPEAKER_00 (host / pro-wealth-tax)
participants.position_summary: Supports a wealth tax on assets over £10m: the country needs revenue, asset appreciation is largely untaxed, billionaires lose nothing meaningful, austerity has demonstrably failed, and entrenched aristocratic fortunes are the real beneficiaries of anti-tax rhetoric.

participants.label: SPEAKER_01 (caller / opponent)
participants.position_summary: Opposes a wealth tax on self-made fortunes: such wealth has already been taxed at 45% income tax, taxing it again is unjust, the wealthy will flee the UK, and government should instead manage existing resources better and reduce waste.

debates.title: If We Tax Rich People... They Will Just Leave!
common_ground: Some wealthy individuals do relocate internationally each year.
The Laffer Curve describes a real relationship between tax rates and revenue, with revenue eventually declining at sufficiently high rates.
Mobility increases with wealth: billionaires relocate more easily than less wealthy individuals.
Capital flight, if it occurred at large scale, would be a genuine economic problem.
Tax policy involves behavioral responses by taxpayers.
Some jurisdictions (e.g., the UK, in absolute terms) have experienced notable outflows of high-net-worth individuals.

participants.label: narrator (SPEAKER_01)
participants.position_summary: Argues that the 'tax the rich and they leave' claim is empirically weak: most countries are on the revenue-increasing side of the Laffer curve, wealthy migration is statistically negligible, homegrown wealth is sticky, and capital flight is typically a symptom of instability rather than taxation. Concedes some flight occurs but argues it is economically minor and that retaining skilled labor matters more than retaining wealthy asset holders.

participants.label: SPEAKER_00
participants.position_summary: Quoted source asserting that 800,000 New Yorkers are prepared to leave the city; functions as a foil for the capital-flight thesis.

participants.label: SPEAKER_02
participants.position_summary: Quoted source citing figures on millionaire and high-net-worth international relocation; functions as foil articulating the capital-flight concern.

debates.title: ‘Preposterous!' LBC callers are divided over proposed wealth tax
common_ground: The UK is a wealthy country in aggregate terms.
The wealthy currently pay tax in the UK, including a 45% top marginal income tax rate.
Some wealthy individuals are leaving or planning to leave the UK.
Public services, including the NHS, are under strain.
Taxing wealth in some form is acceptable in principle (host concedes the principle).

participants.label: SPEAKER_00 (Host)
participants.position_summary: Accepts taxing wealth in principle but argues a wealth tax would drive job-creating entrepreneurs abroad, has failed elsewhere per economists, and that the wealthy already pay substantial tax (45%).

participants.label: SPEAKER_01 (Caller, pro)
participants.position_summary: Supports increased taxation of the wealthy, citing high-happiness countries and the decline in NHS spending as a share of GDP; cites Denmark as a model of high income taxation.

participants.label: SPEAKER_02 (Caller, anti)
participants.position_summary: Opposes treating financial success as morally blameworthy; 'success is not a sin.'

participants.label: SPEAKER_03 (Caller/commentator, pro)
participants.position_summary: Argues Britain is a poor country with rich people in it; wealth has concentrated among billionaires while wages stagnate and public services decline; a wealth tax is needed and capital-flight/workability objections are deflections.

debates.title: The Jacob Rees-Mogg Illusion: Why the Rich Think They’re the Victims - Barry's Economics
common_ground: Rees-Mogg owns a high-value property and possesses substantial personal wealth.
A mansion tax of 1% on property value above £2 million has been proposed.
Rees-Mogg opposes capital/wealth taxation and characterises it as class warfare.
Tax policy should aim to minimise economic damage (a premise the narrator engages with rather than rejecting outright).

participants.label: narrator
participants.position_summary: Rees-Mogg's arguments against wealth/mansion taxes are undermined by vested interest, anchoring bias from elite upbringing, motivated reasoning, and lack of contact with ordinary life; wealth taxation is needed because real wealth accumulation now occurs through assets, not income.

participants.label: Rees-Mogg (reported)
participants.position_summary: Capital taxation is economically harmful and a tool of left-wing class warfare; wealth taxes are administratively too complex; asset-rich/cash-poor households (elderly pensioners, young Londoners with mortgages) would be unfairly hit; tax should target income and expenditure.

participants.label: Rory (reported)
participants.position_summary: Cited as an example of dismissing class-based critique; told Gary that class anger blinded him to political realities.

debates.title: Do you agree with Jacob Rees Mogg's opinion on wealth tax? #clip #fyp #debate #viral #money
common_ground: Billionaires hold very large amounts of wealth relative to others.
Much billionaire wealth is held in illiquid form — company shares rather than cash income.
Billionaires already pay some form of tax.
There is a meaningful and significant disparity between billionaires and ordinary people.

participants.label: SPEAKER_00 (Rees-Mogg)
participants.position_summary: Opposes a wealth tax: argues its real aim is to make people poorer, that billionaire wealth is largely illiquid paper assets, and that support for it is the politics of envy.

participants.label: SPEAKER_01
participants.position_summary: Supports a modest 1% levy on wealth above £1bn as a reasonable, winnable policy, while acknowledging wealth taxes have a poor track record internationally.

participants.label: SPEAKER_02
participants.position_summary: Argues billionaires make substantial profits that could be taxed and challenges Rees-Mogg's framing of 'surplus' wealth.

debates.title: California's proposed wealth tax is 'idiotic concept,' says Tenacity Venture Capital's Narasin
common_ground: California has a record of fiscal mismanagement and waste.
The California wealth tax proposal as currently framed is unlikely to be sound policy.
There is a narrow legitimate concern about founder unrealized gains being borrowed against and passing untaxed at death.
Wealth taxes historically cause some wealthy individuals to leave the taxing jurisdiction.
Taxing wealth that has already been subject to high income tax rates would be confiscatory.

participants.label: SPEAKER_00 (moderator/host)
participants.position_summary: Frames the discussion skeptically toward the California wealth tax, questions Ro Khanna's consistency, and distinguishes confiscatory wealth taxation from a narrower legitimate case around founder unrealized gains and step-up in basis.

participants.label: Narasin (Tenacity Venture Capital)
participants.position_summary: The California wealth tax is an 'idiotic concept' and political pandering; California already wastes existing revenue, and wealth taxes have failed almost everywhere except Switzerland, while triggering wealth flight.

participants.label: Ro Khanna (referenced, not present)
participants.position_summary: Reportedly now supports a 'moderate wealth tax' in California, a shift from earlier positions.

debates.title: Wealth Tax DISASTER? Experts CLASH Over Britain’s Future!
common_ground: The UK currently faces fiscal pressure and questions about how to fund public services.
Some countries that previously had wealth taxes have since repealed them.
Paul Marshall is a very wealthy individual associated with GB News.
Tax policy can influence behavioural decisions of wealthy individuals at the margin.
Changes to the non-dom regime were made by both Jeremy Hunt and Rachel Reeves.
Wealth tax design varies and implementation is non-trivial.

participants.label: SPEAKER_03 (Portes)
participants.position_summary: Wealth is more unequally distributed than income, the very wealthy pay relatively low effective rates, and a 1–2% wealth tax would not cause significant emigration. Personally willing to pay more.

participants.label: SPEAKER_00 (Ibrahim)
participants.position_summary: A wealth tax would disincentivize investment and accelerate millionaire emigration; international experience (most countries repealing wealth taxes) shows it does not work.

participants.label: SPEAKER_01 (host)
participants.position_summary: Frames wealth as earned through risk, hard work, and leveraging; sceptical of wealth taxation; cites international repeal as evidence of failure.

participants.label: SPEAKER_04
participants.position_summary: Objects to personalising the debate around Paul Marshall.

debates.title: Debate: Would a Wealth Tax on the Top 1% Help Tackle Poverty?
common_ground: Tackling poverty and funding public services like the NHS are legitimate policy goals.
Wealth taxes have been tried and abandoned in several European countries (France, Germany, Sweden, Denmark).
Switzerland and Norway currently operate wealth taxes.
The UK currently has significant child poverty and pressures on the NHS.
Tax policy choices affect behaviour, including migration decisions of wealthy individuals.
Fairness is a relevant consideration in tax policy, even if disputed how decisive it should be.

participants.label: SPEAKER_00 (Polanski / wealth tax advocate)
participants.position_summary: Supports a narrow 1% wealth tax on assets above £10m (2% above £1bn), alignment of CGT with income tax, and inheritance tax reform; argues current economy is broken and refusing such reforms is morally indefensible given child poverty.

participants.label: SPEAKER_04 (wealth tax opponent)
participants.position_summary: Argues wealth taxes have empirically failed abroad, cause capital flight and GDP loss, and that economics should be governed by what works long-term rather than fairness.

participants.label: SPEAKER_05
participants.position_summary: Introduces evidence of Spain's wealth tax revenue and presses fairness-based arguments that the very wealthy should contribute more.

participants.label: SPEAKER_02
participants.position_summary: Objects to aligning CGT with income tax on grounds it harms informal/low-income workers lacking employment protections.

debates.title: This Wealth Tax Could Change Everything #taxtherich #tax #economy
common_ground: California's billionaires have seen extraordinary wealth growth in recent years.
Some billionaires will leave or threaten to leave California in response to a wealth tax.
Extreme inequality of wealth and income is a widely acknowledged problem.
The Massachusetts millionaire surtax (2023) generated significant revenue without causing major wealth flight.
The proposed California wealth tax is a one-time measure, not a permanent annual levy.

participants.label: SPEAKER_00
participants.position_summary: Advocates for California's billionaire wealth tax, rebutting objections about flight risk and affordability, and framing it as a step toward broader wealth taxation needed to fund public services and preserve democracy.

debates.title: 'A wealth tax will target ordinary people like me'
common_ground: Ordinary workers should not pay more than their fair share.
There exists a category of 'truly wealthy' (e.g., Duke of Westminster) distinct from ordinary high earners.
A wealth tax with a sufficiently high threshold (e.g. £10m+) would not affect ordinary city workers.
The specific threshold at which a wealth tax applies is the decisive question.
Everyone should pay their fair share of tax — the dispute is solely about where that obligation falls.

participants.label: SPEAKER_00
participants.position_summary: A self-identified ordinary city worker (likely a lawyer) who opposes wealth tax proposals on the grounds that they would capture ordinary high earners rather than the truly wealthy, and distrusts that any threshold would hold under a Labour government.

participants.label: SPEAKER_01
participants.position_summary: Defends wealth tax proposals by pointing out that Kinnock's specific proposal (2% above £10m) would not affect ordinary professionals, and argues everyone must pay their fair share rather than deflecting up the wealth ladder.

debates.title: 'People are so disgusted' - Economist Thomas Piketty on why we need a wealth tax
common_ground: Wealth inequality in the UK and other Western economies is a politically salient issue.
Mainstream politicians have been hesitant to adopt wealth taxes.
The middle class is currently constrained or 'stuck' in the present economic arrangement.
Government inaction on wealth taxation is significantly driven by fear of consequences.
Some form of policy response to wealth concentration is on the political agenda (e.g. mansion tax surcharge).

participants.label: SPEAKER_00
participants.position_summary: Economist (Piketty) arguing that wealth concentration has risen without benefiting average households, that the post-war social contract is collapsing because the rich avoid tax and burdens shift to the middle class and scapegoated groups (poor, migrants, foreigners), and that an annual wealth tax — alongside changed treaties and legal limits on private accumulation — is necessary and feasible.

participants.label: SPEAKER_01
participants.position_summary: Interviewer framing questions about UK inequality, mainstream political hesitancy, the adequacy of Labour's mansion tax surcharge, and challenging the guest with counter-framings (e.g. that reform amounts to tearing up the rule of law).

debates.title: A Wealth Tax Doesn’t Make Sense
common_ground: A wealth tax of the kind proposed by Elizabeth Warren is undesirable.
Illiquid assets like art are difficult to value and tax.
A wealth tax could affect people beyond the very wealthy.
Income tax is a more transparent and understandable mechanism than a wealth tax.
Being forced to liquidate assets in order to meet a tax liability is unreasonable.

participants.label: SPEAKER_00
participants.position_summary: Opposes a wealth tax: argues it is hard to assess non-cash assets, would force asset sales, would trickle down to ordinary people, and would require invasive enforcement.

participants.label: SPEAKER_01
participants.position_summary: Aligned opponent of the wealth tax: questions its mechanism of enactment and characterizes it as a privacy invasion that is nearly impossible to implement.

debates.title: Why a wealth tax doesn’t work | To the Point
common_ground: Many OECD countries tried wealth taxes in the 1990s and subsequently abolished them.
Wealth is difficult to define and value for tax purposes.
Wealthy individuals have the means and tendency to relocate in response to high taxes.
Wealth taxes raise less revenue than initially projected.

participants.label: speaker
participants.position_summary: Wealth taxes are ineffective: OECD experience shows abandonment, revenue underperforms projections, valuation is difficult, and the wealthy relocate to avoid them.

debates.title: Why the Left and Right Are Both Wrong About Wealth - Barry's Economics
common_ground: A wealth tax is, on the surface, commonly perceived as a left-wing idea.
Politicians and political debate tend to frame economic policy through individualist assumptions.
Wealth distribution is a legitimate subject of policy concern.

participants.label: Barry
participants.position_summary: A wealth tax is neither left-wing nor right-wing; it is a question of systemic health. Wealth is not produced or owned in isolation but is a collective product of a healthy society, analogous to calories in a body, and is currently being distributed poorly.

debates.title: Can We Tax The Rich?
common_ground: Wealthy individuals have significant mobility and can relocate their residence.
Under the current system, residence is a primary determinant of tax liability.
The objection that 'taxing the rich will cause them to leave' is a serious concern that must be addressed.
Rich people possess substantial wealth that could in principle be taxed.

participants.label: Gary Stevenson
participants.position_summary: Argues that the common objection to taxing the rich — that they will simply leave — rests on a misunderstanding of what wealth is. Because the rich are rich primarily through ownership of assets (which are locatable), and because the current system ties taxation to residence rather than to assets, the mobility of persons does not preclude effective taxation of wealth.

debates.title: Is a wealth tax actually possible?
common_ground: Designing tax policy correctly is a difficult technical task.
A wealth tax is a policy worth seriously considering.
Wealth inequality is a real phenomenon warranting policy response.
International coordination mechanisms in tax policy are achievable, as shown by existing precedent.

participants.label: Gary
participants.position_summary: Advocates for a wealth tax but argues that designing and implementing it is the proper job of governments, civil services, think tanks, and experts — not of individual campaigners. Concedes UK unilateral implementation would be especially hard, and frames the issue as international, citing the international minimum corporation tax as precedent for coordinated action.

Inherited aristocratic fortunes (Duke of Westminster) as the unearned wealth that should be taxed

Labour's wealth tax is driving wealthy people out of the UK, costing jobs and revenue
Rebutting the 'show me the tax policy' challenge as bad-faith and absurd

Broader stakes: inequality and democracy require taxing the rich
What a good tax system targets — income and expenditure vs assets
Whether opposition to wealth tax reflects self-interest of 'future richer self' vs. a British cultural mentality of making rather than taking
Framing the capital flight argument against taxing the wealthy
Wealth inequality justifies a wealth tax to rebalance an economy that benefits only the super-rich
Analogy to Trump tariffs: amateurish policy is bad; need paid expert tax professionals
Ordinary city workers would bear unfair burden under a wealth tax
Whether Conservatives (vs Reform) are an effective opposition with the right economic strategy
Anti-woke rhetoric functions as a distraction from defending elite wealth
Rebutting the 'billionaires will flee' objection
Outro and call to action
Homegrown wealth is sticky and unlikely to relocate over taxes
Whether Redwood is moving toward Reform party
Rees-Mogg's perspective is distorted because he is disconnected from the experience of real inequality
UK case: wealthy did leave, but they were transient wealth that contributed little
Wealth tax is impractical because valuing non-cash assets is difficult and forces asset sales
Host accepts the principle of taxing wealth but disputes its workability and points to international evidence that wealth taxes fail
Empirical Laffer curve evidence supports high revenue-maximizing tax rates in the US
Specific design questions: capital flight, taxing UK assets, historical lessons
Rebuttal: real wages are stagnant; wealth is now generated through assets, so taxing only income misses where wealth actually accrues
Polanski's rebuttal: assets like land can't move; economists are biased
Inequality and the breakdown of the post-war social contract leading to scapegoating of the poor
Inequality (concentration of wealth) damages growth, democracy, and society
Government should engage him and put in the design work to build a watertight tax
Banter about voting and political parties
Race-to-the-bottom tax competition is a losing strategy
Industry leaving UK due to high taxes and energy/climate policy
Wealth tax enforcement would require invasive privacy violations like registration or door-to-door inspections
Winning narrative debate vs needing others (Zucman, govts) to lead implementation
Continued dispute over renewables manufacturing and industrial strategy
Historical/comparative failure of wealth taxes; competing jurisdictions attract the wealthy
Framing of Rees-Mogg as lacking self-awareness due to privileged upbringing
Closing exchange and sign-off
Wealth tax would change behavior and drive millionaires out of the UK
Framing the debate over introducing a wealth tax in the UK
Considering funding tax experts to work on implementation themselves due to political vacuum
Spanish wealth tax evidence; ethical question of fairness for the very wealthy
Evidence of narrative victory: Kinnock echoing his 2%>£10m proposal; bottom-up public influence
Concession that mobility increases with wealth, but many millionaires are economically preferable to few billionaires
Transition to next guest
Disputing the empirical basis of millionaire-exodus claims and the 16,000 figure
Conventional left/right political division framing
Wealth inequality is an international problem requiring coordinated action across many countries
Rees-Mogg's claim: London professionals with large mortgages on £2m+ homes would be unfairly hit
Wealth is a collective outcome, not individual achievement
Austerity has already been tried for 14 years and failed, justifying a new approach (wealth tax)

Acknowledging difficulty (rich can leave, control media) but countering with Abramovich-style asset taxation
Wealth inequality justifies taxing high-wealth individuals who avoid income tax

Why mainstream politicians resist wealth taxes; international treaty constraints and the social contract argument
Episode introduction and context-setting about Rees-Mogg
Rise of billionaires/trillionaires and the case for highly progressive taxation up to 80-95%
Specifying the wealth tax thresholds and praising scrap of two-child cap
Rebuttal to capital flight claim — wealthy won't leave over 1%; patriotism argument
Capital flight is overstated; moral priority should be doctors/nurses/child poverty over billionaires

Whether human greed motivates flight from taxation, and whether that greed itself justifies taxing them more
Rebuttal to 'they'll leave': assets remain in country and can be taxed; Abramovich/Chelsea example
Challenge: what do the wealthy actually lose by paying a wealth tax, given they would remain the richest?
Dispute over evidentiary status of millionaire departure figures
Justification for wealth tax: country needs revenue, and wealth grows passively without owner effort
Conservative economic strategy: cut waste rather than raise taxes
Personal willingness to pay higher taxes as test of principled commitment
Comedic setup about prison art investment
UK vs US capacity to act unilaterally; international approach preferred
Channel intro
Wealth concentration has risen while middle/lower incomes stagnated; redistribution is politically necessary despite difficulty
Analogy to public health regulation as non-partisan
Rees-Mogg's claim: a wealth/mansion tax would be too complicated and require anti-avoidance mechanisms

Montage of news clips about wealthy people relocating

Mechanism and purpose of the proposed tax
Necessity of taxing wealth to prevent national collapse into poverty; call to mobilise experts

Rich people are rich because they own immobile assets, not because they hold portable cash
Moral priority: needs of millions outweigh complaints of few
Policy design requires teams and time, not one person

Framing the question: if you tax the rich, will they leave?
The blind spot: both sides treat wealth as individually earned
Efficiency-vs-outcome bias / streetlight analogy: complexity is not a valid reason to avoid wealth taxes
Framing: California's wealth tax should be supported
Whether owners of high-value homes are actually wealthy enough to justify a mansion tax
Wealth is now generated from assets rather than income, but assets go untaxed because the wealthy benefit
Redwood's claim that taxing the rich more will drive them abroad (capital flight)
Wealth tax is wrong; alternative revenue sources (e.g. interest tax, targeting bankers) should be used instead

Non-doms / wealthy fleeing to Dubai as a consequence of wealth tax
Whether wealth taxes are an effective policy
International cooperation is achievable and historically reduced inequality (post-WWII model)
Wealth tax proposals risk targeting ordinary high earners rather than the truly wealthy
Explanation for inaction: tax laws are made by very wealthy politicians (Sunak, Cameron, Blair) who have a conflict of interest
Challenge: economists say annual wealth taxes don't work due to behavioural change
Capital flight is a symptom, not cause, of national instability
International examples (Denmark) show high taxation can work; dispute about whether 45% is already enough
Distinguishing confiscatory wealth tax from taxing untaxed unrealized founder gains at death
Whether changing rules means abandoning rule of law; need for new legal limits on accumulation
Whether the rich actually pay their fair share, with dispute over tax avoidance vs. high marginal rates
Thesis: designing wealth tax implementation is the job of experts/government, not YouTubers
Defining the wealth tax proposal: 1% on £10m+ assets, framed as fairness over earned vs unearned wealth
Introduction and thesis preview
Introducing the two guests and inviting Polanski to make the case
Introduction of the wealth tax debate
Wealth tax is political pandering and doesn't work; California mismanages money
Whether public spending (e.g. NHS) needs to rise as a percentage of GDP, justifying more taxation
Wealth tax doesn't work economically — behavioural change, capital flight, evidence from France/Germany/etc.
Introduction of the California wealth tax debate and Ro Khanna's position

Sinking boat analogy: neither side addresses distribution as the real problem
The mansion tax debate is a symptom of broader failure to tax capital properly

Rees-Mogg's hypocrisy/lack of self-awareness in introducing mansion tax discussion from his mansion
Whether billionaires actually have a 'surplus' that justifies taxing them
Fossil fuel subsidies vs renewables / industrial strategy dispute

Everyone deflects the wealth tax onto someone richer; fair share principle

Clarification of 'hoarding'; concern that CGT alignment hurts low-income workers without employment protections
Wealth taxes cause capital/wealth flight, citing France 1989 example
Government fear of international chaos and over-sacralization of property rights/free trade
Wealth tax is wrongly perceived as left-wing; speaker rejects left/right binary
Implementation and design of the tax must be very carefully done
Speaker_02's affiliation with a Reform-aligned think tank
Millionaire migration rates are statistically negligible globally
Sponsor read
Whether wealthy individuals like Paul Marshall earn their wealth through risk/effort and deserve reward
Closing remarks and channel housekeeping
Distrust that Labour would limit wealth tax to the £10m+ threshold (slippery slope)
Calorie/health metaphor: wealth tax is about distribution and societal health, not punishment
Revenue-maximizing rate is not the same as optimum tax rate
Policy mechanism: tax assets located in the country regardless of where the owner resides; forced sales would be desirable

detections.evidence: you might just be asset rich and cash poor
he really truly has that little self-awareness
And what I like about Jacob Rees-Mogg, and when I say like, it's a relative term, but what I like about it is he really truly has that little self-awareness
Rees-Mogg has a direct vested interest in opposing a mansion tax because he lives in a mansion
young Londoners with massive mortgages on a two and a half million pound property
Using a £2m house as the example of a 'working family' home
I think it's because when you grow up in such immense privilege, the normal social boundaries that the rest of us have just don't apply
he has a vested interest in this because he lives in a mansion
what I like about Jacob Rees-Mogg, and when I say like, it's a relative term, but what I like about it is he really truly has that little self-awareness

shared_items.a: The UK faces serious fiscal pressures requiring policy response.
shared_items.b: The UK government faces serious fiscal and public-service pressures that require some response.

shared_items.a: The UK faces serious fiscal pressures requiring policy response.
shared_items.b: The UK currently faces fiscal pressure and questions about how to fund public services.

shared_items.a: Behavioural responses by wealthy individuals to taxation are an empirical question that matters for policy design.
shared_items.b: Tax policy can influence behavioural decisions of wealthy individuals at the margin.

shared_items.a: Child poverty in the UK is a real and significant problem.
shared_items.b: The UK currently has significant child poverty and pressures on the NHS.

shared_items.a: Some form of wealth-related taxation (e.g. a mansion tax) is at least conceivable as a policy option.
shared_items.b: Some form of policy response to wealth concentration is on the political agenda (e.g. mansion tax surcharge).

shared_items.a: Wealthy individuals can and sometimes do relocate to lower-tax jurisdictions.
shared_items.b: Some wealthy individuals do relocate internationally each year.

shared_items.a: The UK currently has a top marginal income tax rate of 45%.
shared_items.b: The wealthy currently pay tax in the UK, including a 45% top marginal income tax rate.
